coredata.dc:title: Preparation of a novel adsorbent with amino-rich carbon quantum dots for efficiently separating Zn(II) from Fe(II) in zinc deplating waste liquid
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Zuopeng
Zhao, Fangbo
Guo, Xinyu
coredata.dc:description: Zinc deplating waste liquid is considered hazardous due to the presence of heavy metal Zn(II) and hydrochloric acid, along with Fe(II). This study aims to develop a new adsorbent for efficient separation of zinc (II) from deplating waste liquid in a real factory. The novel adsorbent, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, was prepared by grafting home-made amino-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) onto a chloromethyl polystyrene resin matrix. It has been observed that amino groups can be protonated under strongly acidic conditions, enabling them to adsorb negatively charged Zn(II)-Cl complexes while repelling positively charged Fe(II)-Cl complexes. The grafted nano-size CQDs-NH2 can offer more adsorption positions to improve the adsorption properties. The results shown that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 presented higher Zn(II) adsorption capacity and better performance in separating Zn(II) from Fe(II) compared to the conventional anion exchange resin (717). At 298 K, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 showed a maximum Zn(II) adsorption capacity of 201.77 mg/g, and a selective Zn(II)/Fe(II) separation factor of 3033.72, which were 54.7% and 45.4 times higher than those of 717 resin. In the fixed bed adsorption process, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited 61.5% higher adsorption performance than 717 resin when treating actual zinc deplating waste liquid. Moreover, the water consumption during regeneration of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 is reduced by 10.3% compared to the 717 resin. These findings indicate that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 is an effective material for the treatment of industrial zinc deplating waste liquid.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots
Surface grafting
Separation of Zn(II)/Fe(II)
Adsorption of Zn(II)

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of CQDs-NH2 concentration on Zn(II) adsorption
Comparison of properties of different types of adsorbents
The effect of chloride ion concentration
The effect of hydrogen ion concentration
Fixed-bed column adsorption and elution curve

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme diagram of methods preparation for CQDs-NH2 (a) and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (b).
Distribution of metal ions (a, b) and UV-Vis spectra of metal chloride complexes (c, d) with 0.001 mol/L metal ions under varying chloride concentrations at 298 K.

FTIR spectrum characterization of PSCL, CQDs-NH2, and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (a). Optical images of CQDs-NH2 and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 taken under UV light (b) and (c). TEM image of PSCL, CQDs-NH2, and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (d-h).

XPS spectra (a). N1s core-level spectra of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (b), and Zn(II)-sorbed PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (e). Cl 2p core-level spectra of PSCL (c), and Zn(II)-sorbed PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (f). Zn 2p core-level spectra of Zn(II)-sorbed PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (d).
Possible mechanism of zinc adsorption process on CQDs-NH2.
SEM and BET images of PSCL (a-c) and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (d-f).
Effect of chloride ion concentration on separation of Fe(II) and Zn(II) (c(Zn2+): 0.3 mol/L. c(Fe2+): 0.5 mol/L. Resin dosage: 5 g. c(H+): 0.5 mol/L. Shaking speed: 150 r/min; temperature: 298 K.).
Effect of hydrogen ion concentration on separation of Fe(II) and Zn(II) (c(Zn2+): 0.3 mol/L. c(Fe2+): 1 mol/L. Resin dosage: 5 g. c(Cl-): 6.1 mol/L. Shaking speed: 150 r/min. Temperature: 298 K.).
Effect of temperature on separation of Fe(II) and Zn(II) (c(Zn2+): 0.3 mol/L. c(Fe2+): 1 mol/L. Resin dosage: 5 g. c(Cl-): 3.6 mol/L. c(H+): 1 mol/L; Shaking speed: 150 r/min.).
Adsorption isotherm and kinetic curve fitting of Zn(II) on PSCL-CQDs-NH2: (a) Langmuir model. (b) Freundlich model. (c) pseudo-first-order model. (d) pseudo-second-order model.

Fixed-bed column adsorption and regeneration curves: (a) breakthrough curves of PSCL-CQDs-NH2. (b) breakthrough curves of 717 resin. (c) breakthrough curves of D301 resin. (d) elution curves of PSCL-CQDs-NH2. (e) elution curves of 717. (f) elution curves of D301.
Formation of zinc and ferrous chloride complexes, and corresponding cumulative formation constants.
BET analysis results of PSCL, and PSCL-CQDs-NH2.
Comparison of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 adsorption capacity with other reported adsorbents.
Effects of co-existing ions on the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) on the PSCL-CQDs-NH2.

head.ce:title: Preparation of a novel adsorbent with amino-rich carbon quantum dots for efficiently separating Zn(II) from Fe(II) in zinc deplating waste liquid

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin, 150001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Greening Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin 150001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Greening Engineering, Harbin Engineering University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Greening Engineering, Harbin Engineering University, Harbin, 150001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc deplating waste liquid is considered hazardous due to the presence of heavy metal Zn(II) and hydrochloric acid, along with Fe(II). This study aims to develop a new adsorbent for efficient separation of zinc (II) from deplating waste liquid in a real factory. The novel adsorbent, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, was prepared by grafting home-made amino-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) onto a chloromethyl polystyrene resin matrix. It has been observed that amino groups can be protonated under strongly acidic conditions, enabling them to adsorb negatively charged Zn(II)-Cl complexes while repelling positively charged Fe(II)-Cl complexes. The grafted nano-size CQDs-NH2 can offer more adsorption positions to improve the adsorption properties. The results shown that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 presented higher Zn(II) adsorption capacity and better performance in separating Zn(II) from Fe(II) compared to the conventional anion exchange resin (717). At 298 K, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 showed a maximum Zn(II) adsorption capacity of 201.77 mg/g, and a selective Zn(II)/Fe(II) separation factor of 3033.72, which were 54.7% and 45.4 times higher than those of 717 resin. In the fixed bed adsorption process, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited 61.5% higher adsorption performance than 717 resin when treating actual zinc deplating waste liquid. Moreover, the water consumption during regeneration of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 is reduced by 10.3% compared to the 717 resin. These findings indicate that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 is an effective material for the treatment of industrial zinc deplating waste liquid.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The steel products undergo pickling with hydrochloric acid before galvanizing, which effectively removes metal oxide on the surface and uneven galvanizing layers [1,2]. However, as the number of pickling cycles increases, efficiency gradually decreases and the solution must be disposed of. The zinc deplating waste solution generated in this process typically contains high levels of Zn(II), Fe(II), and chloride ions, along with small amounts of Fe(III) [3]. Proper handling is necessary to prevent environmental issues and comply with legal requirements [4,5]. Treating and reusing the zinc deplating waste liquid can not only prevent environmental pollution but also yield certain benefits. To achieve this, medium and large factories often employ spray roasting and fluidized bed processes to treat the zinc deplating waste liquid and obtain regenerated hydrochloric acid and iron oxide. While this method allows for resource regeneration, it is only suitable for plating waste solutions with Zn(II) concentrations below 500 mg/L [6–9]. Therefore, for Zn(II) concentrations higher than 500 mg/L, the separation of zinc and iron needs to be carried out first. However, separating Zn(II) from Fe(II) proved to be challenging due to their similar charges and comparable atomic weights, leading to significant resemblance between them.

Various techniques have been developed for the separation of Zn(II) and Fe(II), including adsorption, extraction, and ion exchange [10]. Pietrelli successfully separated Zn(II) and Fe(II) using tri-butyl phosphate (TBP) and di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphoric acid (D2EHPA) as extractive agents [11]. However, this method generated a significant amount of organic wastewater. Miesiac successfully separated Zn(II) from Fe(II) using Lewatit M500 Mono Plus (strong basic anion exchange resin) and Lewatit MP 64 Mono Plus (weak basic anion exchange resin), respectively [2]. However, the research results indicate that the strong basic anion exchange resin exhibits a high adsorption capacity for Zn(II) but is challenging to regenerate, while the weak basic anion exchange resin has a low adsorption capacity for Zn(II) but is easily regenerated. Li et al. discovered that rice straw hydrochars prepared via the hydrothermal method could adsorb Zn(II) with a capacity of 112.8 mg/g [12]. However, its adsorption capacity is limited due to the absence of surface functional groups, leaving room for improvement.

Polyethylenimine (PEI) is a water-soluble polyamine with numerous amino groups [13,14]. Several studies have demonstrated the favorable adsorption capacity of PEI modified materials for Zn(II). For instance, Zhan et al. developed an adsorbent by modifying cellulose/sodium alginate with PEI, which could adsorb Zn(II) up to 110.2 mg/g [15]. Similarly, Pang et al. used PEI to modify magnetic porous material, resulting in a high specific surface area adsorption material with an impressive adsorption capacity of 138.8 mg/g for Zn(II) [16]. However, the large volume of PEI limits its grafting in the pores of the porous material matrix, thus restricting its adsorption capacity due to the lack of surface functional groups. In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained significant attention globally as an environmentally friendly zero-dimensional material [17,18]. CQDs can be easily synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method, facilitating their fabrication [19,20]. Furthermore, compared to PEI, CQDs have an average size of less than 10 nm, providing them with the advantages of high amine content and small size simultaneously [21]. Consequently, CQDs hold promising prospects for utilization as modifiers for porous adsorption materials.

In this work, polyethylene imine into carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) were successfully synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method. A novel adsorbent, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, was synthesized by grafting CQDs-NH2 to the chloromethyl polystyrene resin matrix (PSCL). PSCL-CQDs-NH2 demonstrates a strong positive charge in acidic environments and exhibits high adsorption performance for negatively charged Zn(II)-Cl complexes in solution. Additionally, it shows excellent regeneration performance in fixed bed experiments. The structure of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was characterized using FTIR, TEM, XPS, Zeta potential, and BET techniques. The effects of a series of key factors such as Cl- concentration, hydrogen ion concentration and temperature on the adsorption process were studied by batch method. Different thermodynamic and kinetic models were used to determine the adsorption behavior and mechanism. Finally, to evaluate the practical applicability of PSCL-CQDs-NH2, continuous adsorption experiments were conducted using the fixed bed adsorption method with zinc deplating waste liquid obtained from a steel company in Heilongjiang Province, China. This allowed us to investigate the adsorption and regeneration properties of the new resin.

The Chloromethyl polystyrene (PSCL), 717 [22], D301 [23], D315 [24], and D318 [25] resins were sourced from Zhengzhou Hecheng New Materials Co., Ltd., China. Here, 717 is a strong basic anion exchange resin, while D301, D315, and D318 are weak basic anion exchange resins. Table S1 present their physical and chemical properties, respectively. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (Mw 600 Da), 4-Dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), ZnCl2, FeCl2·4 H2O, LiCl, NaOH, NaCl, anhydrous ethanol, ascorbic acid, and concentrated hydrochloric acid were purchased Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China. The above resins should be pretreated as follows before use. The resins were successively pretreated with saturated NaCl solution, NaOH solution (5%), hydrochloric acid solution (5%) and deionized water until the eluent reached neutral. After that, resins were subjected to extraction with ethanol to remove residue impurities.

10 g of PEI600 was added to a 150 mL round-bottom flask, and the solution was then heated to 175 °C using an oil bath. After heating for 10 min, the obtained solution was immediately dropped-added to 50 mL of deionized water with vigorous magnetic stirring. The obtained solution was then cooled to room temperature and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) for 3 days to remove the remaining molecular precursors. Subsequently, the dialysate was filtered at 1 bar with a PVDF ultrafiltration membrane with a diameter of 0.45 μm to remove large particles. Finally, the dialysate was rotationally evaporated (12 h), oven-dried for 24 h, and freeze-dried for 12 h to obtain a golden solid from CQDs-NH2.

CQDs-NH2 was prepared into aqueous solution with mass fraction of 1%, 10%, 20% and 30%, respectively. 1.0 g of PSCL and 0.05 g DMAP were weighed in a round-bottom flask, and 10 mL of 1, 4-Dioxane was added for 3 h after swelling, 40 mL of CQDs-NH2 solution was added for about 3 h, and the reaction was continued for 10 h at 60 ℃. The product was washed with deionized water until it was free of chloride ions and dried in air blast drying oven at 60 ℃ to obtain PSCL-CQDs-NH2. The reaction mechanism was demonstrated in Fig. 1.

The adsorption spectra of metal chloride coordination compounds were measured by UV-6100 UV-Vis spectrophotometer (Mapada, China). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded using IR Prestige-21 (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). Metal ion concentrations were determined using the ICAP7400 inductively coupled plasma emission spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), at least three duplicated experiments were conducted to obtain the average value under each experimental condition, ensuring the reproducibility and accuracy of the experimental results. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to acquire images of the CQDs-NH2 and PSCL-CQDs-NH2. To observe the surface topography of the adsorbents before and after CQDs-NH2 loading, scanning electron micrographs of the gold-coated samples were taken with a scanning electron microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The chemical composition of the resin surface was analyzed with the ESCALab250xi X-ray photoelectron diffractometer (Thermo Fisher Technologies). The two adsorbents were carried out to N2 adsorption-desorption at 77 K. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the two adsorbents were determined by TriStar II 3020 specific surface area analyzer (Micromeritics, USA). The charges on the adsorbents were determined with a Zetasizer Nano ZSP (Malvern Instruments, Britain) to determine the optimal CQDs-NH2 concentration. The resins impregnated in different concentrations were oscillated in HNY-200D benchtop intelligent constant temperature culture oscillator (Honour Instrument Shaker, China).

The effects of chloride ion concentration, hydrogen ion concentration, and temperature on the separation of Zn(II) from Fe(II) by PSCl-CQDs-NH2 were investigated through batch adsorption experiments. The actual zinc deplating waste liquid obtained from Heilongjiang Jianlong Acheng Steel Co., Ltd., China, were utilized as the feed water, and the concentrations of different elements are presented in Table S2. The LiCl was introduced as a source of chlorine to regulate the concentration of chloride ions, while ascorbic acid was incorporated to inhibit the oxidation of Fe(II) to Fe(III). The flask was placed on an oscillator at 180 r/min for 60 min to ensure the attainment of adsorption equilibrium. Subsequently, a specific quantity of resin was introduced into a simulated solution under controlled temperature and speed conditions. Once adsorption equilibrium was achieved, the adsorbed solution and resin were separately collected and subjected to subsequent analysis. The adsorption equilibrium capacity Q e (mg/g) and adsorption efficiency η (%) were used to describe the adsorption process respectively [26–28]. Eqs. (1) and (2) are the calculation formulas:
(1)
Q
e
= (
c
0
−
c
e
) / V ( 1000 m )
(2)
η =
c
0
−
c
e
c
0
× 100 %
where c 0 and c e represent the initial and equilibrium concentrations of Zn(II) and Fe(II) respectively (mg/L), V (mL) denotes the volume of solution, m represents the mass after drying of resin (g), and the separation efficiency is quantified by the selective separation factor β
Zn/Fe , which is defined as follows [29,30]:
(3)
β
Zn / Fe
=
γ
Zn
/
γ
Fe
(4)
γ
Me
= (
c
0
−
c
e
) /
c
e
Where Me is Zn or Fe, and γ
Me is the ratio of the concentration of metal ions on the resin after adsorption equilibrium to the concentration of metal ions in the solution. Three parallel tests were conducted in each group, and then taking their average values.

Fixed-bed adsorption experiments were conducted to evaluate the separation performance of Zn(II) and Fe(II) using polytetrafluoroethylene column (length 200 mm, diameter 20 mm) wet-filled with quantitative PSCL-CQDs-NH2 or 717 resin equipping with a water bath to maintain constant temperature, respectively. The actual zinc deplating waste liquid were employed as the feeding solution and pumped down-flow through the column at the flow rate of 5 mL/min using the BT100–2 J (Baoding, China) to control the flow rate. The elution experiments were conducted by passing deionized water through a metal-loaded bed column in an up-flow mode, while maintaining a constant temperature using a water bath. The effluent was collected at 10 mL intervals (Samples were collected at a time interval of 5 mL when the effluent zinc ion concentration was 500 mg/L and near the saturation adsorption point), and the concentrations of Zn(II) and Fe(II) were measured.

The reaction formulas for Zn(II) and Fe(II) to form coordination compounds with chloride ions and their corresponding cumulative formation constants were showed in Table 1 [31,32]. It can be seen from the table that Zn(II) has a much stronger ability to form coordination compounds with chloride ions compared to Fe(II). The existence form of metal chloride ion coordination compounds was affected by the concentration of chloride ions at room temperature in the Fig. 2(a) and (b). When the concentration of chloride ions reached approximately 1.75 mol/L, the proportion of Zn2+, ZnCl+ and
ZnCl
2
0
declined rapidly, while the total proportion of anionic coordination compounds gradually increases and reaches 99%. UV-Vis spectra were used to detect the metal ions in solutions with different Cl- concentrations. As shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d), the UV-Vis absorption peak at 262 nm was observed in the zinc-chloride system, and it gradually red-shifted to 272 nm with an increase in the concentration of Cl- from 0 mol/L to 9 mol/L. The UV-Vis absorption peak was considered as
ZnCl
4
2 −
species owing to it being the most stable state in the chloride ion solution [33]. Red shifts in the band maxima can be obviously observed by UV-Vis absorption of Fe(II) when chloride concentration increases [34]. The coordination reaction and equation of metal and Cl- can be expressed as follows:
(5)
Me
2 +
+ n
Cl
−
⇌
MeCl
n
2 − n
(6)
K
n
=
[
MeCl
n
2 − n
]
Me
2 +
∙
[
Cl
−
]
n
where
[
MeCl
n
2 − n
]
, [Me2+], and [Cl-] are the concentrations of the corresponding species, respectively, n = 1 ∼ 4. K is the cumulative formation constant.

By analyzing Zn(II)-Cl and Fe(II)-Cl complexes, the results demonstrate the theoretical feasibility of separating Zn(II) and Fe(II) using an adsorbent with a positive surface charge. In solution, Zn(II) primarily exists as
ZnCl
3
−
and
ZnCl
4
2 −
, whereas Fe(II) predominantly exists as a cationic form. The reaction formula of adsorption of Zn(II) by adsorbent can be represented as follows:
(7)
n − 2
NR
4
Cl
+
MeCl
n
2 − n
⟶
NR
4
n − 2
MeCl
n
+ ( n − 2 )
Cl
−

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CQDs-NH2 concentration on Zn(II) adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: Different CQDs-NH2 concentration had an impact on the properties of the resin adsorbent and its ability to adsorb Zn(II). To investigate the effect on the adsorption capacity of the modified resin, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 were modified using concentration 1%, 10%, 20%, and 30% of CQDs-NH2, respectively. The selection of the most suitable concentration of CQDs-NH2 for modifying the PSCL was based on the Zeta potential of the modified resin and its adsorption capacity for Zn(II). As depicted in Fig. S1 (a), it could be observed that as the concentration of CQDs-NH2 increased, the Zeta potential of the modified resin and its adsorption capacity for Zn(II) reached a maximum at the concentration 20%. Therefore, the concentration 20% was selected for subsequent tests.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), FTIR was used to analyze the PSCL, CQDs-NH2, and PSCL-CQDs-NH2, respectively. The spectrum of CQDs-NH2 exhibited an obvious broad absorption peak at 3272 cm−1, attributing to N-H vibration. The peaks at 1585 cm−1 represented the N-H deformation vibrations of amino. In addition, the peaks at 1659, 1359, and 756 cm−1 were the typical peaks of PEI. When PSCL was grafted on CQDs-NH2, the N-H vibration absorption peak was shifted and appeared near 3400 cm−1. In addition, typical peaks of PEI were observed at 1659,1585, and 1395 cm−1 compared to PSCL. As shown in Fig. 3 (b) and (c), both the synthesized CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 were able to exhibit uniform blue light under UV lamp (365 nm). The morphology and diameter of CQDS-NH2 were analyzed by TEM, as shown in Fig. 3 (d), (e), and (h). CQDs-NH2 had a uniform distribution and spherical shape with a particle size of about 5 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 (f) and (g), TEM comparison shows that CQDs-NH2 is encapsulated within the PSCL matrix phase, forming nanoclusters. Therefore, the above structures indicate that CQDs-NH2 was successfully grafted on PSCL.

In order to further validate the immobilized CQDs-NH2 on the PSCL, XPS is utilized, the fine spectrum of PSCL, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, and Zn(II)-sorbed PSCL-CQDs-NH2 are exhibited in Fig. 4. In Fig. 4(b), N1s is fitted to two characteristic peaks at N-H (399.1 eV) and C-N (400.7 eV) [35,36]. Table S3 presents the XPS element content analysis of PSCL and PSCL-CQDs-NH2. The results demonstrate a significant increase in the N element content, while the Cl element content has nearly disappeared. This suggests that the active sites on CQDs-NH2 and PSCL have undergone complete reaction. After PSCL-CQDs-NH2 adsorbed Zn(II), the peak of 401.4 was presented as protonated N of CQDs-NH2, indicating the N element on CQDs-NH2 was protonated in acidic environment [14,37]. As shown in the Fig. 4(d) and (f), Zn(II)-sorbed PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited new peaks corresponding to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 (197.5 and 198.9) as well as Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 (1021.6 and 1044.7), respectively. Based on the aforementioned observations, as well as the fact that Zn(II) exists mainly as
ZnCl
3
−
and
ZnCl
4
2 −
in the presence of high chloride ions, it can be inferred that Zn(II) is predominantly adsorbed in its anionic form by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 ( Fig. 5) [33].

The morphological changes of CQDs-NH2 and PSCL-CQDs-NH2 were characterized using SEM images, as shown in Fig. 6 (a), (b), (d), and (e). PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited a significantly improved surface roughness compared to PSCL. Numerous convex points were observed on the surface of PSCL-CQDs-NH2, and the pore channels of the resin were noticeably reduced. These observations indicate the successful modification of CQDs-NH2 on the PSCL.

Fig. 6(c) and (f) presents the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherm as well as the pore size distribution diagram for PSCL and PSCL-CQDs-NH2. Analyzing the isotherm, the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms of the samples exhibited a type-IV adsorption isotherm with a significant hysteresis loop, indicating that the sample has mesoporous and macroporous structures. Table 2 demonstrates that the grafting of CQDs-NH2 on both the PSCL led to a concurrent reduction in specific surface area, average pore size, and pore volume. This trend could be attributed to the CQDs-NH2 molecules dispersed into the pore diameter were grafted with the inner wall of the pore, which further reduced the pore diameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of properties of different types of adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: PSCL-CQDs-NH2, and 4 commercial resins were tested for Zn(II) adsorption (c(Zn2+): 0.3 mol/L, c(Fe2+): 1 mol/L, Resin dosage: 5 g, c(H+): 1 mol/L, c(Cl-): 6.1 mol/L, shaking speed: 150 r/min and temperature: 298 K). These commercial resins adsorb negatively charged Zn(II)-Cl complexes in an acidic environment. Table S4 shows the Zn(II) adsorption performance of 5 adsorbents. The adsorption capacity of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 for Zn(II) exceeded that of commercially available strong basic and weak basic anion exchange resins. In order to compare the adsorption capacity of PSCL-CQDs-NH2, 717 resin will be used as a reference sample as it has the highest Zn(II) adsorption capacity among commercially available resins. The adsorption capacity of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was compared to several other reported adsorbents, as presented in Table 3. It is evident that the adsorption capacity of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 is higher than that of the majority of other adsorbents with good adsorption capacity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of chloride ion concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
As observed in Fig. 7, the PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited a higher adsorption capacity for Zn(II) compared to 717 resin. Table S5 indicated that the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 increased by 51.86% compared to 717 resin. As the chloride concentration increased from 2.37 mol/L to 7.02 mol/L, the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 also increased from 181.97 mg/g to 201.57 mg/g. The adsorption efficiency of Zn(II), and β
Zn/Fe gradually increased from 89.78% to 99.45% and from 219.56 to 1677.02, respectively. This can be attributed to the increased presence of
ZnCl
3
−
and
ZnCl
4
2 −
in the solution, which facilitated the capture of Zn(II) by the active sites on the resin. However, it is important to note that solid adsorbents may be affected by the "Donnan invasion" effect, where a small amount of metal cations can penetrate into the resin during reversible chemical reactions between the aqueous and solid phase ions [42]. As a result, both resins exhibited some adsorption of Fe(II), particularly when the chloride ion concentration reached approximately 5 mol/L. At this concentration, Cl− coordinates with Fe2+ to form
FeCl
4
2 −
, which is subsequently adsorbed by the resin, leading to an increase in the adsorption capacity of Fe(II) [34]. Therefore, in subsequent experiments, the chloride ion concentration was maintained below 5 mol/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of hydrogen ion concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
The effect of hydrogen ion concentration in solution on the adsorption of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was investigated, and the results were illustrated in Fig. 8. According to Table S5, the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 were increased by 57.63% and β
Zn/Fe was increased by 45.38 times compared to 717 resin. The adsorption efficiency of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 for Zn(II) was achieved at a hydrogen ion concentration of 2.0 mol/L, resulting in a maximum adsorption capacity of 201.77 mg/g and β
Zn/Fe of 3033.71. However, the adsorption efficiency decreased when the hydrogen ion concentration exceeded 2.0 mol/L. For hydrogen ion concentrations below 2.0 mol/L, the increase in Zn(II) adsorption efficiency on PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was attributed to the protonation of hydrogen ions with the nitrogen element on the adsorbent. However, beyond this point, the excess hydrogen ions may compete with the adsorption sites, thereby reducing the capture of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2. Therefore, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was found to be suitable for use at hydrogen ion concentrations below 2 mol/L.

As shown in Fig. 9, the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) on both adsorbents decreased with increasing temperature. Table S5 reveals that the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was 51.11% higher compared to 717 resin. For PSCL-CQDs-NH2, as the temperature rose from 278 K to 328 K, the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) decreased from 195.64 mg/L to 175.44 mg/L, corresponding to a decrease in adsorption efficiency from 96.52% to 86.56%. β
Zn/Fe also decreased from 1362.13 to 321.91. This phenomenon can be attributed to the increase in effective heat energy, which enhanced the mobility of adsorbents and promotes desorption. Moreover, higher temperatures lead to increased solubility of metal ions in the liquid phase when water was used as the solvent, intensifying the competition between the solvent and adsorbents.

In order to provide a more detailed explanation of the adsorption mechanism of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2, the following experiments were designed to conduct adsorption isothermal experiments. A 50 mL volume of a solution containing Zn(II) (ranging from 65 to 13,000 mg/L) and chloride ion (5 mol/L) was added to a 100 mL conical flask. Then, 1 g of resin was added and the mixture was allowed to contact for 1 hour at a temperature of 298 K. Two adsorption isothermal models, namely Langmuir and Freundlich, were used to assess evaluate the saturated adsorption capacity and adsorption performance of the resin for Zn(II). The formulas for the two models are as follows:
(8)
Q
e
=
Q
m
∙
C
e
b +
C
e
(9)
Q
e
= k ∙
C
e
n
where Q e (mg/g), C e (mg/L) and Q m (mg/g) are the adsorption equilibrium mass of Zn(II), the concentration of Zn(II) in the solution at equilibrium and the maximum adsorption amount at adsorption equilibrium, respectively. b is Langmuir adsorption constant (mg/L). k and n are the two Freundlich constants related to the adsorption capacity and adsorption behavior, respectively. n is an important index that can evaluate the suitability of this model in the Freundlich formula, when n > 1, the model is not suitable for such adsorption. When 0 < n < 1, the model fits this adsorption model. When n = 0, it indicates that the adsorption is irreversible.

Fig. 10 and Table 4 demonstrated that the Langmuir model exhibited the highest correlation coefficient R 2 (R 2 > 0.991), indicating superior fitting performance compared to the Freundlich model at various temperatures. The results indicated that the adsorption of Zn(II) and Fe(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 was homogeneous in monolayer [43]. According to the data in the table, the theoretical maximum adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by the resin was determined to be 369.08 mg/g. Table S6 shows that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited a favorable adsorption effect on Zn(II).

As depicted in Fig. S1(b), the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDs-NH2 exhibited rapid augmentation within the initial 30 minutes, followed by a gradual decrease in the adsorption rate. After 70 minutes, the adsorption reached a point of saturation, with a maximum saturation adsorption capacity of 289.86 mg/g. This phenomenon could be attributed to a gradual reduction in the availability of effective binding sites on the PSCL-CQDs-NH2 over time. Considering energy consumption and ease of operation, the optimal contact time was determined to be 70 minutes. Tables 4 and 5 present the results of adsorption kinetics analyses utilizing the quasi-first-order and quasi-second-order models, respectively.
(10)
ln
Q
e
−
Q
t
= ln
Q
e
−
K
1
t
(11)
t
Q
t
=
1
K
2
Q
e
2
+
t
Q
e
where K 1 (min−1) and K 2 (g·mg−1·min−1) represent the rate constants of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models, respectively. The fit picture of Zn(II) is shown in the figure, and from R 2 the fitting pseudo-second-order model gives the best fit. The adsorption rate was mainly controlled by chemisorption, which indicated that the adsorption behavior may be related to the valence potential of shared valence electrons between metal ions and PSCL-CQDs-NH2.

In addition to Zn(II) and Fe(II), the waste liquid produced by different galvanization plants may also contain small amounts of Ni(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) [6,10]. Therefore, it is necessary to consider the effect of these interfering ions on the adsorption properties of PSCL-CQDS-NH2. Ni(II), Cu(II), and Pb(II) were added in the actual waste liquid (c(Zn2+): 0.3 mol/L, c(Fe2+): 1 mol/L, c(H+): 1 mol/L, and c(Cl-): 4.2 mol/L), in order to test their effects on the adsorption performance. Table 6 shows that the adsorption capacity of Zn(II) by the adsorbent slightly decreased after adding these three ions, indicating that the addition of interfering ions had no significant effect on the adsorption of Zn(II) by PSCL-CQDS-NH2. This result aligns with previous reports [14], suggesting that PSCL-CQDS-NH2 has the potential to effectively treat actual zinc deplating waste liquid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fixed-bed column adsorption and elution curve
ce:section.ce:para: In this experiment, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, 717, and D301 resins were employed for dynamic adsorption and regeneration tests (Fig. S2). The concentrations of Zn(II) and Fe(II) were evaluated using an actual zinc deplating waste liquid from a steel company in Heilongjiang Province, China, with concentrations of 6.561 g/L and 111.76 g/L, respectively. Each 50 mL of resin was added to the Teflon column using the wet column loading method. Fig. 11(a-c) show the breakthrough curves for PSCL-CQDs-NH2, 717, and D301. To adhere to the limitations of the spray baking method, it is crucial to ensure that the zinc plating waste liquid contains a zinc ion concentration of no more than 500 mg/L. Therefore, the adsorption performance standard for the adsorbent was set at the effluent zinc ion concentration of less than 500 mg/L. The effluent volume of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (about 420 mL) was much greater than that of 717 (about 260 mL, a difference of more than 61.5%), and D301 (about 210 mL). Moreover, the effluent volume of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 (690 mL) was higher than that of 717 (about 530 mL), and D310 (about 460 mL), when the resin adsorption reached full saturation. Fig. 11 (d) demonstrates the elution curve of PSCL-CQDs-NH2, with resin regeneration achieved at an effluent volume of 290 mL, which was less than that of 717 (about 320 mL, less than 10.3%, Fig. 11(e)), and more than D301 (about 270 mL, Fig. 11(f)). Therefore, PSCL-CQDs-NH2 could be regenerated with only a small amount of deionized water, and although the amount of deionized water used was slightly higher than that of weak basic anion exchange resin, its adsorption capacity was much higher than that of strong basic anion exchange resin.

The main sources of fouling in the zinc deplating waste solution were carbon and silicon on the surface of steel, as well as dirt on the operation site. The results presented in Fig. S3 demonstrate that the adsorption efficiency of the adsorbent remained above 99% of the initial state after several adsorption-desorption cycles of the filtered waste liquid, indicating the excellent stability of the adsorbent. However, when treating the unfiltered zinc deplating waste liquid, the adsorption efficiency of the adsorbent after several adsorption-desorption cycles was 96% of the initial state. The decrease in adsorption efficiency caused by the zinc deplating waste liquid after pretreatment was significantly lower than that of untreated waste liquid. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fouling material attaching to the surface of adsorbent materials during the operation of the fixed bed, which leads to contamination and renders the active sites on the surface of the adsorbent materials ineffective. Therefore, it is crucial to filter the waste liquid before processing it.

A novel modified resin, PSCL-CQDs-NH2, synthesized by grafting CQDs-NH2 to the PSCL, which exhibited a positive charge in an acidic environment. It exhibited a positive charge in an acidic environment. It was found to be effective in the separation of Zn(II)/Fe(II) due to the amine-rich and nano-size properties of CQDs-NH2, and its positive charge during protonation under low pH conditions. The adsorption experiments and theoretical analyses showed that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 had a 54.7% higher adsorption amount of Zn(II) and a 45.4 times higher separation coefficient of Zn(II)/Fe(II) compared to 717 resin. The Langmuir adsorption model revealed that the monolayer adsorption capacity of PSCL-CQDs-NH2 for zinc ranged from 347.6 mg/g to 369.1 mg/g at 278 K and 318 K. Furthermore, the adsorption kinetics analysis revealed that the process was predominantly governed by chemisorption. This phenomenon was attributed to the effect of electrostatic attraction resulting from the protonation of the abundant amine groups immobilized on the PSCL under strong acid conditions. XPS results also shown that zinc ions were adsorbed by PSCl-CQDs-NH2 mainly in the form of Zn(II)-Cl complexes. The fixed bed adsorption experiments demonstrated that PSCL-CQDs-NH2 had a higher Zn(II) adsorption capacity compared to the conventional strong basic anion exchange resin, while also exhibiting easy regeneration properties similar to those of weak basic anion exchange resin. Consequently, the PSCL-CQDs-NH2 demonstrated excellent potential for removing Zn(II) ions from zinc deplating waste liquid.

Zuopeng Guan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Conceptualization. Fangbo Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xinyu Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there is no conflict of interests regarding the publication of this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51979064), Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (LH2023E068), Heilongjiang Provincial Key R&D Plan Projects of China (GA23C005) and Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities of China (3072022QBZ1005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2024.112488.
